preview_text:
The model favors Japan to win at 71%, with Tunisia assigned only an 8% chance of victory. Japan enters as a clear favorite, though the market assigns the underdog nearly twice that probability.

The model sees this as a competitive but asymmetric contest. A draw at 21% is the second-most likely outcome, suggesting reasonable defensive structure from Tunisia despite the overall gap in win probability. The 63-percentage-point spread between Japan's win chance and Tunisia's reflects a substantial but not overwhelming model edge.

The model and sportsbooks diverge notably here. The market consensus—drawn from 43 books—assigns Tunisia 18%, nearly 10 points above the model's 8%, and reduces Japan's win probability to 55%, a gap of 16 points. Polymarket prices align more closely with the model on draw likelihood (26%) but also lean toward Tunisia relative to the model's assessment. The dashboard does not adjudicate between these sources; the gap reflects genuine disagreement on the likely outcome.
